claimed_contribution: The paper is presented as the first to examine consumers' relinquishment of decision control to surrogates through the self-regulatory strength framework.
It proposes and tests a psychological mechanism in which delegation threatens self-esteem as a free agent, and coping with that threat depletes self-regulatory resources.
It claims to advance freedom-of-choice research by showing that losing choice autonomy can impair later self-control, and by identifying free-will self-affirmation as a boundary condition.
context: The paper studies consumer decisions in which control may be relinquished to surrogates such as travel agents and physicians. Evidence comes from controlled scenarios (hotel and medical treatment choices) and reminder-based tasks about past delegated versus independent decisions, including a shopping context.
design_and_data: The authors report four experiments. Study 1 (N = 53 undergraduates, lab) manipulated independent versus delegated hotel choice and measured depletion as the drop in a key-press persistence task from before to after the decision. Study 2 (N = 98 online panelists from Canada, UK, Australia, and New Zealand) used a 2 x 2 design (decision mode x free-will affirmation) in a medical-treatment scenario and measured depletion via dessert choice (chocolate cake vs fruit salad). Study 3 (N = 36 Canadian online panelists) and Study 4 (N = 107 U.S. online panelists) used reminder manipulations of past delegated versus independent decisions; Study 4 separated forced and voluntary delegation and measured depletion with susceptibility to sales promotions in a simulated convenience-store purchase task.
main_findings: Delegating a decision produced greater self-regulatory depletion than making the same decision independently, even though participants reported lower decision effort under delegation.
Affirming participants' belief in free will attenuated the depletion effect of delegation, as shown by reduced impulsive dessert choice in the delegation condition when affirmation was present.
Simply recalling past delegated decisions increased depletion relative to recalling independent decisions, and this held for both forced and voluntary delegation reminders in the final study.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, the paper finds that delegating consumer decisions to surrogates can deplete self-regulatory resources more than independent choice, with free-will affirmation reducing this effect and reminders of past delegation reactivating it.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work on ego depletion and choice autonomy by showing that actual decision delegation to surrogates can be more depleting than independent choice despite lower effort, and by demonstrating self-affirmation and memory reminders as key moderators of this effect.
research_gap: Prior consumer research had examined source selection and forced loss of choice, but had paid limited attention to the psychological processes involved when consumers delegate decisions to surrogates. The paper also argues that earlier autonomy-depletion findings did not directly test delegation or specify the underlying mechanism.
research_question: Does delegating a consumer decision to a surrogate deplete self-regulatory resources more than making the same decision independently, even though delegation is less effortful? The paper also asks whether this effect is due to a self-esteem threat from relinquishing free agency, such that free-will self-affirmation dampens it and reminders of past delegation can recreate it.
